refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Periodically sample material quantity at fixed time intervals, compute differential changes, and extrapolate to period-level throughput estimate
DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time
DP4: Establish reference material batches of known quantity, measure batch processing rate under representative conditions, and scale to full-period throughput projection
DP5: Decompose throughput into constituent process stages (e.g., feed rate, processing rate, discharge rate), measure each independently, and aggregate via process model to infer system throughput
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time

potential_sources: Boundary definition coupling: Input and output measurement points must be spatially and temporally well-defined to avoid double-counting or loss attribution ambiguity
Accumulator synchronization: If input and output counters operate asynchronously, clock drift or timing misalignment could introduce systematic bias
Material hold-up: Transient material in-process (e.g., in hoppers, conveyors) creates interpretation ambiguity—net throughput reflects steady-state only if hold-up is constant
Measurement independence: Input and output sensors must be decoupled in sensing principle to avoid correlated failure modes
coupling_check.notes: DP3 exhibits low functional coupling because input and output measurement are logically independent; failure of one does not directly compromise the other. Temporal coupling is manageable via synchronized time-stamping. Material hold-up is a known systematic effect, not a coupling defect. Recommended mitigation: establish clear boundary definitions in IM layer and validate hold-up stability during acceptance testing.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material properties (density, moisture, particle size) remain stable during evaluation period. Risk: Variation in density or moisture content can cause mass-to-volume conversion errors if only one modality is measured.
Assumption: Input and output measurement points are accessible and do not interfere with normal operations. Risk: Retrofitting sensors may require process shutdown or safety re-certification.

Assumption: Accumulator counters are calibrated and drift is negligible over evaluation period. Risk: Sensor drift or calibration decay could introduce systematic bias; periodic re-verification required.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria threshold (productivity_target) is achievable under representative operating conditions without process modification. Risk: If target is unattainable, measurement system will correctly report failure, but root cause (process design vs. measurement error) must be disambiguated.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation and operation of measurement instrumentation without additional hazard controls. Risk: Explosive atmospheres, high-temperature zones, or moving machinery may constrain sensor placement and require certified intrinsically safe designs.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and establish steady-state throughput. Risk: Short evaluation windows may not capture diurnal or operational variability; recommend minimum 1–4 hour continuous operation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) whether throughput is measured as net (output − input) or gross (output only, assuming negligible hold-up change); (2) acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., ±5% of target); (3) required temporal resolution (instantaneous vs. period-average); (4) whether material property variation (density, moisture) must be compensated or is assumed constant. Additionally, clarify whether 'representative operations' includes transient startup/shutdown phases or only steady-state. This will constrain DP selection and IM realization (e.g., real-time data logging vs. batch reporting) and reduce ambiguity in acceptance testing.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized material flow monitoring and throughput computation subsystem that instruments input/output boundaries, maintains independent accumulation counters, and derives net throughput metrics normalized by elapsed time.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed net throughput, accumulation counter states, and material flow metrics to external monitoring/logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive configuration parameters for counter initialization, boundary definitions, and throughput computation intervals

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts if throughput deviates beyond safe operating thresholds or counter anomalies are detected

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: material flow instrumentation, accumulation logic, and throughput computation are core NGHE functions. Counter state management and time-normalized calculations are naturally localized. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces require external coupling, but this is expected and minimal.

Poor containment: Vehicle module typically owns propulsion, navigation, and structural systems. Material flow instrumentation is orthogonal to vehicle dynamics. High diffusion: would require Vehicle to host measurement logic unrelated to its primary function, creating tight coupling with NGHE-specific concerns.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation owns remote command/control and operator interface logic. Material flow throughput computation is a domain-specific analytics function, not a control or interface concern. High diffusion: would scatter measurement logic across teleoperation pathways.

Weak containment: Safety Services owns hazard detection and alert generation. While SafetyAlert interface is used, the core DP (accumulation counters, throughput computation) is not a safety-specific function—it is a material flow analytics function that Safety Services would consume, not own. Diffusion: counter state and computation logic would be misplaced in a safety module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally a material flow measurement and analytics function that belongs in the NGHE (Next-Generation Handling Equipment) Solution domain. NGHE is responsible for material handling operations, including input/output boundary management, throughput optimization, and operational metrics. Accumulation counters and net throughput computation are core to NGHE's operational intelligence. The DP exhibits high cohesion within NGHE and minimal diffusion because all measurement, state, and computation logic can be encapsulated in a single subsystem module.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is itself a distributed or federated architecture, counter state synchronization across multiple NGHE instances could introduce coupling complexity. Mitigation: ensure counter state is centralized or use eventual consistency patterns with clear ownership.

Risk: Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces create outbound dependencies. If these interfaces are not stable or undergo frequent changes, the DP module may require frequent updates. Mitigation: define interface contracts clearly and version them.

Risk: Tasking interface for configuration may introduce tight coupling if task scheduling logic is tightly bound to counter initialization. Mitigation: decouple configuration from task scheduling via a dedicated configuration service.

Risk: Time synchronization across distributed sensors at input/output boundaries could introduce clock skew errors in elapsed-time normalization. Mitigation: implement robust time-sync protocol or use monotonic elapsed-time counters.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The DP specifies a clear solution approach: instrument boundaries with independent counters, compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time. This logically satisfies the requirement to enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time. The DP is solution-agnostic (does not prescribe specific sensor types or communication protocols) and is verifiable.

diffusion_risks: Counter state synchronization: If NGHE Solution is distributed across multiple instances or nodes, accumulation counter state must be centralized or use eventual consistency patterns. Risk of clock skew in elapsed-time normalization if input/output boundary sensors lack synchronized time references. Mitigation: implement monotonic elapsed-time counters or robust time-sync protocol.

Interface stability: Telemetry and SafetyAlert outbound interfaces create external dependencies. Frequent changes to these interface contracts could force DP module updates. Mitigation: establish versioned, stable interface contracts.

Configuration coupling: Tasking interface for counter initialization and boundary definition could introduce tight coupling if task scheduling logic is intertwined with counter setup. Mitigation: decouple via dedicated configuration service with clear separation of concerns.

Minor diffusion in SafetyAlert and Telemetry coupling: While expected and minimal (diffusion_score 0.08), these outbound dependencies should be monitored for creeping responsibilities (e.g., SafetyAlert should not pull computation logic into Safety Services; it should only consume alerts).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment within NGHE Solution (containment_score 0.92). Material flow instrumentation, accumulation logic, and throughput computation are cohesive and naturally localized to NGHE's operational domain. Counter state management and time-normalized calculations are core NGHE functions. Alternative candidates (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) exhibit poor to weak containment (0.25–0.40) and high diffusion (0.60–0.75), confirming NGHE Solution as the correct ownership boundary. The IM boundary is well-defined and aligned with the DP.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion ('reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period') is directly measurable via the DP outputs (net throughput metrics). NGHE Solution's Telemetry interface enables transmission of accumulation counter states and computed throughput to external monitoring systems, supporting empirical validation during representative operations. Time-normalized throughput can be logged and compared against productivity_target. Worksite safety plan and operational policies can be verified through SafetyAlert interface outputs and counter anomaly detection.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution via DP3 with explicit module boundaries, interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry out, SafetyAlert out), and responsibilities (boundary instrumentation, counter maintenance, throughput computation, time normalization). Ownership is unambiguous. Configuration parameters flow in via Tasking; metrics flow out via Telemetry; safety deviations flow out via SafetyAlert. No allocation ambiguity or orphaned responsibilities detected.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly specify time synchronization strategy in DP3 or in a supporting design note: either synchronized clocks across input/output boundary sensors or monotonic elapsed-time counters within NGHE Solution to eliminate clock skew risk in throughput normalization.
Define Tasking interface contract (configuration schema, initialization sequence, boundary definition format) to decouple counter setup from task scheduling and reduce coupling risk.
Establish versioned interface contracts for Telemetry and SafetyAlert to stabilize external dependencies and reduce maintenance burden.
If NGHE Solution is distributed, document counter state ownership model (centralized vs. eventual consistency) and synchronization protocol to mitigate distributed state risks.
Add acceptance criterion detail: specify units (mass/volume per unit time), measurement precision/tolerance, and evaluation period duration to support verification against productivity_target.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM boundary is well-contained (0.92 containment score), and verification is feasible. However, the gate results expose critical implementation risks—particularly distributed counter state synchronization, clock skew in time normalization, and interface stability—that warrant explicit decomposition into sub-FRs to ensure these risks are addressed systematically rather than deferred to detailed design. Given high safety criticality, deriving sub-FRs for state management, time synchronization, and interface contracts will improve traceability and risk mitigation. This is a controlled zigzag: we proceed downward to decompose DP3 into sub-level FRs that isolate and resolve the identified diffusion and coupling risks.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Enable accurate measurement of elapsed time across material flow boundaries without clock skew or synchronization failures.
FR3.2: Maintain consistent accumulation counter state across NGHE Solution instances (if distributed) or ensure monotonic counter integrity in single-instance deployments.
FR3.3: Provide stable, versioned interface contracts for Telemetry and SafetyAlert to decouple external dependencies and reduce maintenance coupling.
FR3.4: Support initialization and configuration of material flow boundaries and counter parameters via Tasking interface without tight coupling to task scheduling logic.
FR3.5: Detect and report counter anomalies (e.g., state corruption, synchronization failures, boundary definition errors) to Safety Services via SafetyAlert for operational oversight.

revise.why: DP3 is logically correct but lacks explicit specification of time synchronization strategy and counter state ownership model. Recommend adding design notes or sub-DP specifications that address: (1) monotonic elapsed-time counter implementation or synchronized clock protocol; (2) centralized vs. eventual-consistency counter state model for distributed deployments; (3) Tasking interface schema for boundary definition and counter initialization. These clarifications will reduce ambiguity and support verification of sub-FRs.

assumptions: NGHE Solution may be deployed as single instance or distributed across multiple nodes; counter state ownership model must be defined before implementation.
Input/output boundary sensors can be instrumented with independent counters; sensor availability and placement are feasible within worksite constraints.
Tasking interface is available and can be extended to support counter initialization and boundary configuration without disrupting task scheduling.
Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces are stable or can be versioned to support long-term maintenance.
Productivity_target is defined in units (mass/volume per unit time) and evaluation period is specified in operational requirements.
open_risks: Distributed counter state synchronization: If NGHE Solution spans multiple nodes, eventual consistency or centralized state management must be chosen and validated. Risk of data loss or inconsistency if synchronization fails. Mitigation: derive FR3.2 sub-FRs to address state ownership and synchronization protocol.

Clock skew in time normalization: If input/output boundary sensors lack synchronized time references, elapsed-time calculation may be inaccurate, leading to incorrect throughput metrics. Mitigation: derive FR3.1 to specify time synchronization strategy (monotonic counters or clock sync protocol).
Interface coupling: Telemetry and SafetyAlert interface changes could force DP module updates. Mitigation: derive FR3.3 to establish versioned, stable interface contracts.

Configuration coupling: Tasking interface for counter initialization could introduce tight coupling with task scheduling. Mitigation: derive FR3.4 to decouple configuration from scheduling via dedicated configuration service.

Counter anomaly detection: No explicit mechanism specified for detecting counter state corruption or synchronization failures. Mitigation: derive FR3.5 to add anomaly detection and SafetyAlert reporting.
evidence_needed: Time synchronization strategy specification: document choice of monotonic elapsed-time counters vs. synchronized clock protocol; validate against clock skew tolerance in throughput calculation.
Counter state ownership model: if distributed, specify centralized vs. eventual-consistency approach; provide synchronization protocol and failure recovery procedure.
Tasking interface contract: define configuration schema, initialization sequence, and boundary definition format; verify decoupling from task scheduling logic.
Telemetry and SafetyAlert interface versioning strategy: establish versioned contracts and deprecation policy to stabilize external dependencies.
Acceptance criterion detail: specify units (mass/volume per unit time), measurement precision/tolerance, evaluation period duration, and counter anomaly thresholds.
Verification plan: outline testing approach for time synchronization, counter state consistency, interface stability, and anomaly detection under representative operational conditions.
